notes.title: Resurrection Reshapes Time
notes.content:
For Karl Barth, the resurrection of Jesus Christ does not just prove something about Jesus. It reshapes the meaning of time itself.

Ordinary time: the closed circle. Normally we think of time as a continuous line governed by sin, decay, and death. Nothing escapes that pattern. History is closed.

The resurrection interrupts that timeline. Barth says the resurrection is not simply one event within the timeline — it is an interruption from outside. God's eternal life enters the sequence of human history. Something happens in the middle of history that does not belong to history's normal rules. It is like a window opening from eternity into time.

The future breaks into the present. The resurrection represents the future of God's kingdom arriving early. The new creation has begun, even though it is not yet fully visible.

Why Christians live in two times: Because of this, Christians live in a strange tension. We still exist in ordinary history. But we also belong to the reality revealed in the resurrection — between the old world still marked by death, and the new world revealed in Christ.

Why this produces hope instead of optimism: Barth strongly distinguishes hope from optimism. Optimism believes history is gradually improving through human effort. Hope believes God has already acted in Christ, and therefore the final future is guaranteed by God — not by human progress.

Hauerwas draws heavily from Barth here: the church is a people who live according to the story of Christ rather than the story of the world. Christians organize their lives around the event that has already changed time: the resurrection. The resurrection also reveals the true meaning of the cross — without it, the cross looks like a failure. With it, it becomes the decisive victory of God.

notes.title: Impossible Possibility
notes.content:
Describing what happens in the resurrection of Jesus Christ.

Resurrection is not just unlikely, but fundamentally impossible. Why? Because everything about human history is governed by sin, decay, death. Human beings cannot overcome these forces. No moral effort, religion, or philosophy can conquer death.

So when the resurrection happens, it breaks every rule that structures human existence. From the perspective of history, biology, and human capability, it simply cannot happen!

Yet it actually happens.

That is the shock of the gospel: God does not work within the boundaries of human possibility. God acts from beyond them. So what is impossible for humanity becomes possible because God acts.

Because the resurrection is the "impossible possibility," it cannot be reduced to proof. It confronts us as a decision. Either we dismiss it as impossible, or we trust that God has acted. Faith is therefore not intellectual certainty but trust in God's action.

notes.title: Romans 1 Barth
notes.content:
"For the invisible things of God are clearly seen."

The insecurity of our whole existence, the vanity and utter questionableness of all that is and of what we are lie as in a text-book open before us.

When humans try to grasp God through religion, philosophy, morality, or culture, God's first response is "No." Why? Because every human attempt to capture God turns Him into something manageable — an idea, system, or projection.

We see God by seeing His invisibility. What we truly perceive is that God cannot be grasped by us. God transcends our knowledge. God cannot be reduced to human concepts. God remains hidden. The most truthful knowledge of God is knowing we cannot possess Him.

Think about the resurrection: Jesus is NOT present in a controllable, visible way. The risen Christ CANNOT be held or managed. He appears and disappears. God is revealed precisely as the One who cannot be contained.

Pattern of Gospel:
1. Human pride — we think we know God.
2. Divine KRISIS — God exposes (judges) that illusion.
3. Human humility — we realize we cannot reach Him.
4. Divine grace — God reveals Himself anyway.

So the gospel begins with God saying "No." But that No makes possible a far greater Yes. Barth thinks the gospel must begin with judgment because only when our illusions about God collapse can we finally receive the God who reveals Himself in grace.

notes.title: Predestination (Election of Grace)
notes.content:
Election is not the terror of Christian thinking in every age, but a pervasively gracious election — the sum of the gospel.

1. It is the self-determination of God at the beginning of all his movement beyond himself that brings about fellowship with the human race. As such, it carries with it the determination of that race for fellowship with God.

2. It reaches its fulfillment in time with the consummation of the covenant with Israel in Jesus Christ. It is the divine election of fellowship with sinners in which he consigns the judgment of sin to himself and life to the sinner. It is witnessed to the rejected for their salvation by the "one community" which has the double form of Israel and the church of Jews and Gentiles.

3. Recipients of election are the rejected sinners whom God has chosen as his covenant partners in Christ, as the "one elect community" bear witness.

notes.title: Trinity
notes.content:
Father = Revealer
Son = Revelation
Spirit = Revealedness

We know God as triune in the sense that God is the Subject, Act, and Goal of revelation.

The Trinity is not a speculative puzzle but the grammar of God's self-disclosure. God is not first "God in general" who then chooses to reveal himself — rather, God just is this act of revelation in three modes. The one who reveals, the revelation itself, and the event of being revealed are not three separate things but one God.

notes.title: Sin
notes.content:
Presume we know what sin is before knowing what redemption is.

This short note carries a penetrating implication: we must understand redemption first — who God is and what God has done in Christ — before we can properly understand sin. Sin is not a self-evident human category that we bring to the gospel. Rather, it is defined by grace.

We don't know how far off course we were until we see where we were supposed to be going. The cross reveals sin precisely because the resurrection reveals what God's "Yes" looks like.

notes.title: Ethics
notes.content:
Dogmatics includes ethics because the God of grace is also the God who commands.

Barth refuses to separate theology from ethics. God's grace is not merely a spiritual reality but a claim on how we live. Ethics is not an add-on to doctrine but embedded within it — the gracious God who saves is the same God who calls us to live accordingly.

This is why Christian ethics cannot be reduced to applying universal moral principles — it must always begin with the particular God revealed in Jesus Christ, and ask: what does it mean to live before this God?

notes.title: Analogy of Faith
notes.content:
To demonstrate God cannot be proved on the basis of his being thought, for it is impossible to think of him as merely being thought. "Fides quaerens intellectum" = faith seeking understanding.

Knowledge does not begin with the I who knows — the intellectus must follow after the fides.

Intellectus comes about by reflecting on the Credo that has already been spoken and affirmed. In the Analogia Fidei, in correspondence to what is believed to be true, as discipleship on the level of thought.

Humans do not already have the truth possessed in themselves to posit or possess the truth of God. Rather, in the encounter with God, humans receive the engagement with the truth so that then one may seek to relate to it appropriately in the search for knowledge.

We cannot know God apart from faith that God's disclosure of truth (Jesus) calls forth. This faith that perceives the truth (Jesus) as true, thus acknowledges him and responds to him. Thus we can think of him only because he has given himself to our thinking as the truth. He IS NOT God simply because we have thought of him thus.

Faith Precedes Knowledge: knowledge then has to do with the one true and authentic subject that it cannot ever ground or prove or posit.

You encounter Jesus, you commit to Him (faith), and only then does the real knowledge of who Jesus is begin to unfold. God is the Subject — He is the one doing the acting, the speaking, and the grounding. We don't verify God; God verifies us.

We aren't trying to prove the Gospel is true; we are trying to live in a way that shows we actually believe it is the truth that grounds everything else.

notes.title: Dialectic Theology
notes.content:
Can only speak of God paradoxically — why?

We cannot presuppose God because God always presupposes God's self over against humanity. Thus we speak of God dialectically.

We are human, however, and cannot speak of God. We ought therefore to recognize both that we should speak of God and yet cannot, and by that very recognition give God the glory.

Key insight: "We cannot talk about God, because only God talks about God. Because God does this, however, we can then do it in our own way, although not as something we should do."

Hence "The Word of God" becomes the center of theology — Christ as The Word of God addressed to man.

Barth shows how our attempts to define God are problematic because such attempts presume that God can be captured by our language and knowledge of the created world. We cannot presuppose God because God is the Subject, not the object.

In the Church, we are the ones being interrogated. God presupposes Himself — He is the beginning, the middle, and the end of the story. Our job is simply to be the people who show up and realize we've been invited into a conversation that was going on long before we arrived.

The theme of Christianity is the fulfilling of the relationship of God who sets himself in relation with us based upon the divine initiative, namely the covenant proclaimed in both the OT and NT. We can speak of God only as we also speak of ourselves as those to whom God addresses and claims.

notes.title: Word
notes.content:
The Word of God is the reality that God has made and does make himself known to us in the concrete history of Jesus Christ. Thus the WoG is not simply an idea that we can know or possess or realize, but rather it is identical with the reality that God himself speaks in what is attested and proclaimed.

WoG contests the idea that humanity possesses the possibility of knowing God. This is not self-evident to the human person that the true God is hidden from him — thus this must be said to him by the WoG. Thus we say: "God conceals Himself in revealing Himself."

God is concealed even in his revelation. Revelation as concealment means that revelation remains a possibility of God alone. Without the divine concealment understood in this way, humanity cannot in fact have dealings with God in his revelation.

The fact that in God's real speaking humanity begins to deal with God is made possible by God alone — there is NO possibility for this present within the human.

notes.title: Faith and Reason
notes.content:
Barth refuses a strong distinction between faith and reason. Don't take unbelief too seriously — because if you need an account of faith that can defeat your unbelief, faith is no longer gift but achievement.

The difference between Christian and non-Christian is knowledge (noetic), and that is not an achievement but a gift. Therefore providing an account of belief/knowledge is to treat it as an achievement, not gift.

Faith is knowledge and an ongoing rational endeavor, so refuse the distinction between faith and reason. Try to avoid using "faith" and instead use "faithfulness."

Nonfoundationalist view of truth: doesn't try to establish a theory of truth prior to having necessity to say what is true.

If Jesus is what we mean when we speak of God, why are we surprised the world looks the way it is?

notes.title: NFTU 1
notes.content:
Notes from the Underground, Part 1.

"I hated saying: 'Forgive me, papa, I won't do it again' — not because I was incapable of saying it, but, on the contrary, perhaps precisely because I was all too capable of it!"

Dostoevsky's Underground Man identifies a trap: the more you demand a single "primary cause" that ends all doubt, the more your thinking generates an endless "cause behind the cause," which produces paralysis rather than action.

Heightened consciousness yields "inertia" (a deliberate sitting with folded hands) because reflection keeps reopening the question of foundations. Dostoevsky contrasts this with "men of action," who through limitation treat immediate, secondary causes as primary ones — and therefore feel "at ease," which is what allows action to begin.

Infinite regress problem: if every reason needs another reason, justification appears to run "to infinity," so you never reach the restful foundation you were promised.

One standard way to stop regress is foundationalism: some beliefs are "basic" (not justified by further beliefs) and can support others. In a Christian key, this often shows up as treating belief in God as rationally basic — arguments can still help, but aren't always required for rationality.

notes.title: NFTU Stone Wall
notes.content:
The "Stone Wall" of Evidence (2×2=4):

The Underground Man identifies the "laws of nature" and mathematical certainty as a "stone wall." For a rationalist, the stone wall is a comfort — it is truth, reality, and the foundation of action.

The Apologetic Parallel: This is the apologist who believes that if they can just present the "Stone Wall" of the Resurrection evidence or the Cosmological Argument clearly enough, the non-believer must surrender.

The Underground Critique: The Underground Man admits the wall is there, but he refuses to reconcile himself to it simply because it is true. He declares: "2×2=4 is a piece of impudence... 2×2=5 is sometimes a very charming thing too."

A rationalist apologetic assumes the intellect commands the will. The Underground Man demonstrates that the will is sovereign over the intellect. A human being can see the truth, acknowledge it is the truth, and still reject it out of sheer spite — just to prove they are free and not a "piano key" played by the laws of logic.

Dostoevsky is fighting for freedom. He critiques "Systematic Theology" or theodicies that try to create a "Crystal Palace" of faith — a system where every Bible verse fits perfectly, every instance of evil has a tidy explanation.

As Dostoevsky implies, the only force strong enough to break the loop of the Underground Man is not a better argument, but active love — a force that bypasses the "2×2=4" logic of the world entirely.

notes.title: Dosto Freedom
notes.content:
If faith were merely "rational," it would just be another mathematical proof that you are forced to accept. Dostoevsky hated this idea because it destroys human freedom. If God proved Himself to us mathematically, we would have no choice but to believe — and therefore our love for Him would not be free.

Therefore, faith is not an intellectual deduction, but an act of "active love."

Father Zosima teaches: you cannot prove God exists through argument. You can only prove it by actively loving your neighbors. The more you love, the more convinced you become of the reality of God.

Faith is not rational; it is existential — a free, willful choice to embrace active love and meaning in a world that logic alone can only diagnose as broken.

For Barth, faith is absolutely not a human work — it is purely a gift of God's grace. Faith is the miraculous event where the Holy Spirit opens a person's eyes to acknowledge the objective revelation of God in Jesus Christ.

For Dostoevsky's characters, active love is the method by which one attains faith. You love, and therefore you believe.

For Barth, this is completely backward. You cannot love your way into believing in God. Instead, God reveals Himself to you, granting you faith by grace, and because of that faith, you are freed to participate in active love.

Barth would cheer Dostoevsky's smashing of the rationalist "Crystal Palace." However, he would warn that replacing "intellectual deduction" with "existential active love" is just replacing the idol of human reason with the idol of human emotion and will. For Barth, true faith looks entirely away from the self and entirely toward the external, objective revelation of Jesus Christ.

notes.title: Three Temptations
notes.content:
Over and over Dostoevsky emphasizes that mankind is confronted with the inevitable choice between freedom and bread. The central idea of "The Legend of The Grand Inquisitor" is that mankind can be fed only at the expense of individual freedom. Laws of nature (logic, mathematics, rationalism) is our bread, and thus slavery.

Miracle, mystery, and authority are the three forces that can ensnare the conscience of the weak.

Temptation 1 (Bread): By refusing to turn stones into bread, Christ refused to found faith on guaranteed material security. The Inquisitor thinks people will sell freedom for bread every time.

Temptation 2 (Mystery): Mystery is the attempt at forcing God's hand to make truth a certainty and unavoidable, so it is obvious. This circumvents discipleship, for it attempts to see what is true rather than undergo the training and habits that allow us to see rightly.

Romans 1 describes a world already transparent enough to make rebellion culpable. The second temptation offers a world so transparent that rebellion becomes impossible. That is exactly why Jesus refuses it.

Revelation must have taken place in order for there to be rejection. The issue is not whether revelation happens, but that revelation demands discipleship.

Temptation 3 (Power): Jesus refusing to be another power that mirrors the powers of the world — refusing Caesar's sword.

The silence and kiss suggest that Christ's answer is not a counter-argument but a different logic entirely: love that refuses to violate freedom, even to save people from their own weakness.

The story sets two visions of humanity against each other: one that treats people as children who must be kept safe through miracle, mystery, and authority, and another that treats them as persons who must be allowed to risk suffering, doubt, and even damnation in order to love and believe freely.

Why the father let the prodigal son leave: he did not treat the son like a child that needs to be saved from himself, but gave him the dignity of risking rejection and suffering in order for the love between father and son to be truly free.

notes.title: Father, Tell Me, Why Must I Love You?
notes.content:
"Father, tell me, why must I love you? Father, show me that I must love you. If that father is able to answer him and show him good reason, we have a real, normal, parental relation, not resting on mystical prejudice, but on a rational, responsible, and strictly humanitarian basis. But if he does not, there's an end to the family tie."

Why should Dmitri love his father after all the suffering he has endured from him? This mirrors Jesus on the cross: "Father, why have you forsaken me?" Why shouldn't Jesus in turn forsake the Father and refuse the cup and come down from the cross? Why shouldn't Dmitri refuse his cross that he would suffer though innocent?

Dmitri did NOT wrong his father, as Jesus did not wrong the Father. So why should Dmitri or Jesus suffer for wrongs they did not commit? Even more pressing, why should they love their father?

The moment you call moral law "prejudice," you remove the ground on which forgiveness itself stands. Because if nothing is truly wrong, nothing can truly be forgiven. Thus: all is permissible.

Onion parable = Salvation is communal. The moment you try to be saved alone, you lose the very thing lifting you.

notes.title: The Devil
notes.content:
The intellect can map reality. It cannot give you a reason to suffer for it.

And when the soul has no reason to suffer, it settles into ironic detachment. That's boredom.

The cure is not less thinking. It's thinking that is embedded in a way of life — prayer, love, community, obedience, sacrifice.

Boredom is what happens when the mind tries to be sovereign. Are you pursuing understanding as mastery — or as participation in something larger than yourself?

That difference determines whether intellect becomes wonder... or weariness.

This is what happens when intelligence is left to itself.

notes.title: The Idiot
notes.content:
What happens to God when He enters the world without power, without coercion, without usefulness?

Prince Myshkin is not "God," but he is a Christ-figure stripped of triumph. He does not win arguments. He does not organize goodness. He does not outmaneuver evil. He is beautiful in a way that is socially disastrous.

Not the God of control, but the God who risks humiliation.

This is the scandal of the incarnation taken seriously — not a God who wins by worldly metrics, but one who loves in a way the world cannot account for and therefore cannot protect.

notes.title: Matthew 18
notes.content:
The church does not exist to provide an ethos for democracy or any other social structure of the world. Rather, it exists to be an alternative to every nation, witnessing to the kind of social life made possible by a people formed by the story of Jesus Christ.

"The Church doesn't have a social ethic; the Church is a social ethic."

One of the biggest mistakes the Church has made was thinking it needed to seize the reins of the State/Democracy to make sure "Christian values" were upheld. This is Constantinianism — the desire to be "effective" rather than "faithful." Christians are tempted to rule like tyrants, believing that we possess the truth, a universal truth available without needing to undergo discipleship. But we cannot know the truth that is Christ without undergoing the training to be his disciple.

Peacemaking means choosing the right conflicts. Often we prefer to just leave offenses alone because then we know that this or that person is my enemy, and we often make sense of ourselves based on who our enemies are. Matthew tells us we must confront one another — and so we must face the reality that when confronting the person who offended you, we face the difficult fact that we might need to forgive them.

Any nonviolence that is not shaped by truthfulness is probably just order but not peace. Peace is a deep ongoing activity that challenges us to the bone.

notes.title: Sermon on the Mount
notes.content:
NOT mere truths that can be abstracted from the one who preaches, as if these are an innate part of humanism.

The Father has refused to let our refusal determine our relationship with him. We are, therefore, being trained to ask through the sermon that Jesus delivers, and the asking is part of the way of life that makes it possible for us to be befriended by God and one another.

Those who follow him will necessarily be exposed to dangers that those who are not his disciples can avoid. His disciples offer the world an alternative to the violence of the world based on the lies thought necessary for people to survive in a world governed by mistrust. In such a world, a people of truth cannot help but be in danger because the world does not want its lies exposed.

We are often more defined by what we hate or dislike than what we love or desire. Yet Christians are called to be peacemakers, which means they need to confront the conflict that may arise between people. Being a peacemaker is not simply about ignoring the difficulties, but addressing them rather than ignoring them just to be "nice."

"My sense of who I am is more determined by what I'm against, rather than what I'm for. I'll pray that God can have my loves, but not take my hates. Right. If you take my hates, how will I know who I am?"

notes.title: Separation of Church & State
notes.content:
Jesus is not recommending that we learn to live with divided loyalties. Rather, he is saying that all the idolatrous coins should be sent back to Caesar, where they belong. Just as Jesus knows no distinction between politics and religion, neither does he know any distinction between politics, economics, and the worship of God.

"No one can serve two masters; for a slave will either hate the one and love the other, or be devoted to the one and despise the other. You cannot serve God and wealth" — nor can you serve God and the emperor.

That God and the emperor cannot both be served is not solved when the emperor is said to be "the people." The people often turn out to be more omnivorous in their desire for our loyalty than emperors. Nor is the problem of loyalty to God and Caesar solved by the separation of church and state. That separation too often results in legitimating the state to do what it wants while sequestering the church into the mythical realm of the private.

Christians are usually Herodians but lack the means to recognize themselves as such.

notes.title: Fasting
notes.content:
For those called to be disciples of Jesus, fasting is not dismal, though it may be hard, but rather fasting becomes a way of life that can be called joy. To fast is to discover the gifts that make our lives livable. To fast is to learn not to despise our bodies destined even to death. So to be drawn into a life of fasting is to learn to live without what I assumed I could not live without.

True ascetics often deny that they are ascetics because they do not think their suffering to be that significant. Rather, they discover that their suffering is the source of freedom. Nothing enslaves more than that which we think we cannot live without. Fasting involves the discovery of who we serve.

Just as God supplied Israel daily with bread in the wilderness, so followers of Jesus have been given all they need in order to learn to depend on one another on a daily basis. Without the community that Jesus has called into existence, we are tempted to hoard, to store up resources, in a vain effort to insure safety and security.

Jesus is good news to the poor, for he has brought into existence a people who ask for no more than their daily bread.

To fast means learning how to live without asking for anything more than our daily bread — not idleness, but a community of trust. Such a community offers the hope that habits can be developed that draw us away from the forms of greed justified in the name of security.

That we are now able to live freed of possession is because the one has come who alone has the power to dispossess us.

notes.title: Language of Sacrifice
notes.content:
Sacrifice is the preeminent human action that embodies our desire to return all that is to God. God created us to be animals that sacrifice. God instructed Israel through the law to sacrifice to him, but Israel proved, as we all prove, capable of turning God's good gift of sacrifice into a device to serve the presumption that we are our own creator.

In Jesus, God joined his life to our life, becoming one of us, to free us from the attempt to determine our relationship to him on our terms. The Father sent the Son, humbled in human form, obedient to the point of death, even the death on a cross, to end forever any sacrifice not determined by Jesus's cross.

Our Father restrained Abraham, providing a ram in place of Isaac, but did not spare his only Son from becoming for us the sacrifice necessary to free us from our endless attempts to atone for our sins on our own terms. This is the forgiveness of sins we receive when we eat and drink with Jesus.

notes.title: Tempter
notes.content:
Our effort to live without risk not only results in injustice, but it also makes our own lives anxious, fearing that we never have enough. In truth, we can never have enough if what we want is the bread that the devil offered Jesus. But Jesus is good news to the poor, for he has brought into existence a people who ask for no more than their daily bread.

Jesus feeds the Israelites and they obey while they are given bread, but what about when he ceases to give them bread? It is a transactional relationship, NOT free love. Freedom is not freedom that is bought by providing bread.

Jesus also does not buy our freedom by creating a spectacle like coming down from the cross so that all would see and believe. The temptation is to get Jesus to rule the way we want him to rule over us — having God rule over us in the way we want to be ruled.

We want our bread, we want to force God to rescue us and be in relationship with us on our own terms, and we want peace now even if it is by the sword of Caesar rather than waiting in suffering in our Christlike lives. We are impatient.

The trick that Eve did not recognize is to try to answer the devil on the devil's own terms. The devil's question was designed to suggest that Eve and Adam could go behind the word of God and establish for themselves what the word entailed — assuming they were equal with God.

The devil's temptations are meant to force Jesus to acknowledge that our world is determined by death. Death creates a world of scarcity — a world without enough food, power, or life itself. But Jesus resists the devil because he is God's abundance.

notes.title: Satisfaction?
notes.content:
Any account of the cross that suggests that God must satisfy an abstract theory of justice by sacrificing the Son on our behalf is clearly wrong — indeed, dangerously wrong. The Father's sacrifice of the Son and the Son's willing sacrifice is God's justice. Just as there is no God who is not the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit, so there is no God who must be satisfied that we might be spared. We are spared because God refuses to have us lost. Such is God's justice.

For Hauerwas, drawing deeply on Karl Barth, sin is not just a debt — it's a distortion of our ability to live truthfully with God and one another. You don't fix that by "balancing the books." You fix that by being made new.

Satisfaction-based justice tempts us toward violence. If justice requires satisfaction, then someone must pay — and we are very quick to decide who. That logic underwrites everything from retributive criminal systems to war. It gives us moral permission to harm in the name of restoring order.

The cross is what happens when God refuses to abandon a violent world. It is God's nonviolent, truthful presence in the face of our need to make someone "pay." The resurrection then exposes that entire economy of satisfaction as a lie. God doesn't get justice by settling scores — God creates a people capable of living without needing to.

So justice, for Hauerwas, is not about satisfaction but about rightly ordered relationships made possible by forgiveness.

notes.title: Judgement
notes.content:
God's judgement is gracious judgment because in it, God separates sinners from their sins but doesn't separate himself from sinners, instead defining and claiming them as his own.

This is the heart of it: judgment is not abandonment. God does not judge us by walking away. He judges us by drawing near and refusing to let sin define us. The sinner is not condemned but claimed. The separation is between the person and their sin — not between God and the person.

This is why election and judgment belong together in Barth: to be elected is to be judged (your sin is taken seriously and borne by Christ) and to be freed (you are not abandoned to your sin but claimed as God's own).

notes.title: Love
notes.content:
Christianity is a religion of love and so you try to do the greatest good for the greatest number. And so Jesus becomes the symbol of love but in service of a utilitarian ethic.

The problem is such a love does not have the necessary discerning judgment of what it is you cannot do.

People tend to replace the cross with a utilitarian love, thinking that when all is set and done we just need to love one another — a generalization of love that is dangerous.

No, we need to love each other rightly. Love without the cross, love without judgment and discernment, is not the love of God but a sentimental substitute. The cross is what love looks like when it takes sin — and the other person — seriously enough to not look away.

notes.title: Language
notes.content:
Scripture never defines God, but renders God via narrative — not abstract definitions.

God is not a concept to be defined but a character revealed through a story. The narrative of Scripture — from creation to covenant to Christ — is itself the mode of divine self-disclosure. Definitions abstract, but narrative particularizes and locates.

This connects deeply to Barth: God cannot be captured by our language or concepts because God is a living subject who speaks, acts, and narrates himself into our history. The Bible's refusal to "define" God is not a failure of philosophical precision — it is theological faithfulness.

notes.title: Time?
notes.content:
The resurrection of Jesus Christ means that Christians cannot think about time the way the world does.

The resurrection is not merely a happy ending to a tragic story. It is God's invasion of the world. It is the moment when God refuses to let the world's story — sin, violence, and death — have the final word. The world assumes death is the last chapter. God raised Jesus from the dead to show that death is not even the final paragraph.

That means history has already been interrupted. The resurrection is God's declaration that the future — in which God will redeem the whole creation — has already begun in the middle of history.

Christians therefore live between two times. We live in the old time of the world, the time that still bears the wounds of sin. But we also live in the new time revealed in the resurrection — the time in which God has already begun to make all things new.

The church exists to make that new time visible. When Christians forgive one another, when they refuse to answer violence with violence, when they care for the weak and the forgotten, they are not simply being morally admirable. They are living as people whose lives have been reordered by the resurrection — living according to the future.

If the resurrection is true, Christians should live like people who already know how the story ends: choosing forgiveness instead of revenge, caring for the weak instead of chasing power, trusting God rather than fearing death.

notes.title: Time
notes.content:
Hauerwas rejects the idea that we simply live in 2026, glancing back at something that happened in 30 AD.

Christ's life defines time itself.

The resurrection is not just something that happened in history — it reorders history. When you are baptized, when you worship, when you receive Eucharist, you are not reenacting something. You are being brought into Christ's ongoing life. Not memory. Participation.

When Hauerwas says we participate in Christ's time, he is very Augustinian. Time is not neutral space — it is narratively structured by God's action in Christ.

notes.title: To Be Human
notes.content:
To be human means that we recognize that our lives are constituted first and foremost by God's refusal to let us destroy ourselves. This requires the recognition of our vulnerability which we often try to hide even from ourselves.

To be human is not to be self-sufficient, autonomous, or self-creating. It is to be the kind of creature that is held — by God, by others — even when we resist being held. Our humanity is discovered not in self-mastery but in the acknowledgment that we need.

notes.title: In Truth We Stand with the Pilates…
notes.content:
In truth we stand with the Pilates of this world. We want God to be a king with armies. We do not want to give up our understanding of God as the one capable of putting everything right on our terms. We do not want Jesus to be abandoned because we do not want to acknowledge that the one who abandons and is abandoned is God.

We seek to explain these words of dereliction, to save and protect God from making a fool out of being God, but our attempts to protect God reveal how frightening the God of Jesus Christ is. That God rightly frightens us.

Yet God is most revealed when he seems to us the most hidden: "Christ's moment of most absolute particularity — the absolute dereliction of the cross — is the moment in which the glory of God, his power to be where and when he will be, is displayed before the eyes of the world."

notes.title: The Sermon Cannot Be Abstracted from the Preacher
notes.content:
The Sermon on the Mount is unintelligible if it is isolated from its context within Matthew's gospel. The Sermon on the Mount cannot help but become a law, an ethic, if what is taught is abstracted from the teacher.

"The sayings of the Sermon on the Mount are the interpretation of Jesus's life, and that same life is the necessary condition for the interpretation of the sermon."

"The sermon, therefore, is not a list of requirements, but rather a description of the life of a people gathered by and around Jesus. To be saved is to be so gathered."

"Action in accord with Christ does not originate in some ethical principle, but in the very person of Jesus Christ."

Hauerwas's key methodological insight: once the sermon is severed from Jesus, it becomes a set of ideals we either try to live up to (and fail) or soften into principles. The hard sayings stop being hard — they become either law that condemns or ideals we conveniently set aside. Only when the sermon is read as the self-description of the one who embodies it does it remain gospel rather than another moral demand.

notes.title: Repentance: Thinking Differently About the Kingdom
notes.content:
"To repent is not to feel bad but to think differently."

"The kingdom does not come through our trying to be better people. Rather, the kingdom comes, making imperative our repentance."

"To repent is not a prophetic call for those who repent to change the world, but rather he calls for repentance because the world is being and will be changed by the one whom John knows is to come."

"Live differently, moreover, means that the status quo can be challenged because now a people are the difference."

John baptizes in the Jordan as a reminder of Israel's baptism in Exodus — he calls Israel again to face death that it might live. Repentance is not self-improvement; it is a death-and-life reorientation around the one who is coming. The kingdom is not achieved by moral effort — it arrives and creates the urgency for transformation. The community that repents becomes the embodied difference in the world.

notes.title: Jesus's Temptations: Politics, Worship, and Scarcity
notes.content:
"He understands, as is seldom acknowledged particularly in our day, that politics is about worship and sacrifice."

"By rejecting the devil Jesus calls into the world a people who, as Augustine suggests, can never obey the justice the world offers as an alternative to the justice found in the sacrifice of the Son."

"The devil's temptations are meant to force Jesus to acknowledge that our world is determined by death. Death creates a world of scarcity — a world without enough food, power, or life itself. But Jesus resists the devil because he is God's abundance."

"There is enough food, power, and life because the kingdom has come, making possible a people who have the time to feed their neighbors."

"The devil is but another name for our impatience. We want bread, we want to force God's hand to rescue us, we want peace — and we want all this now."

Each temptation is an offer of a political alternative to the cross: feed the masses into loyalty, force God's hand into spectacle, rule through domination. Jesus refuses all three because they represent ways of ruling through scarcity, fear, and coercion. His kingdom is an alternative politics grounded not in managed scarcity but in the abundance of God.

notes.title: The Beatitudes: A Community Formed for the Cross
notes.content:
"Paul does not assume that our poverty of spirit is the same as Jesus's self-emptying, but rather that Jesus's poverty has made it possible for a people to exist who can live dispossessed of possessions."

"Such a people are capable of peacemaking because they are sustained by the purity derived from having no other telos but to enact the kingdom embodied in Jesus."

"They are an alternative to the violence of the world that is too often called 'peace.'"

"It is the same patience that animates those blessed in the Beatitudes, for they are examples of the kind of people who have the time in God's patience to care."

The Beatitudes are not a spiritual checklist or a personality profile. They describe the shape of a community called into existence by Jesus: a people who have been dispossessed of the world's securities and found themselves, paradoxically, freed to be agents of God's peace. Poverty of spirit is not depression — it is the recognition that we are held by grace rather than our own striving.

notes.title: The Lord's Prayer as Political Act
notes.content:
"To pray that God's will be done is to pray that our wills be schooled to desire that God's will be done. Our wills, the will of the world, will nail Jesus to the cross. But God defeated our willfulness, making it possible for us to pray that God's will be done on earth."

"Just as God supplied Israel daily with bread in the wilderness, so followers of Jesus have been given all they need in order to learn to depend on one another on a daily basis."

"To learn to have our sins forgiven, indeed to learn that we are sinners needing forgiveness, is to become part of the kingdom of God. To forgive and to be forgiven is not some crude exchange bargain to 'get on with life,' but rather to participate in a political alternative that ends our attempts to secure our existence through violence."

"Prayer is perfect activity because it is done for no other purpose than itself."

The Lord's Prayer is not a private devotional exercise. Each petition is a political statement: "our" Father displaces all earthly lords; daily bread challenges systems of hoarding; forgiveness breaks the cycle of retributive justice. Prayer forms a people whose desires are reordered around God's agenda rather than the world's economics of scarcity.

notes.title: Discipleship vs. Admiration: Following to the Cross
notes.content:
"'I follow him to the cross, but not on the cross. I'm not getting myself crucified.' 'Then I don't believe you're a disciple. You're an admirer of Jesus, but not a disciple of his.'"

"Jesus does not ask us to believe in him; rather he asks his disciples to follow him — even unto death."

"Satisfaction theories of the atonement dominate accounts of Christ's work, making it possible for the 'saved' to avoid the radical character of the discipleship depicted in Jesus's sermon."

"Jesus be accepted as one's 'personal savior,' which is then thought to make possible the attempt to follow the teachings of the Sermon on the Mount — 'cheap grace.'"

"The new David is not one whose purple is immediately evident, but rather his power can be found only in his crucifixion."

Cheap grace is the great enemy of discipleship: salvation as a transaction that leaves us unchanged, Jesus as personal savior who asks nothing of us structurally. Hauerwas insists that to follow Jesus is to be constituted as part of a body whose very existence is shaped by the cross — not merely to hold correct beliefs about it. Admiration stays at a safe distance; discipleship is dragged along.

notes.title: The Church Between Two Ages
notes.content:
"That Jesus has been victorious means that the time of the church, the time constituted by those called by Jesus into his kingdom of forgiveness, is characterized by the coexistence of two ages."

"The present aeon is characterized by sin and centered on man; the coming aeon is the redemptive reality which entered history in an ultimate way in Christ. The present age, by rejecting obedience, has rejected the only possible ground for man's own well being; the coming age is characterized by God's will being done."

"The difference between church and world is not a given, but rather a difference between agents. The difference between church and world is not that of realms or levels, but of response."

"Prior to Constantine it took exceptional conviction to be a Christian. After Constantine it takes exceptional courage not to be counted as a Christian."

Drawing on John Howard Yoder, Hauerwas insists the church exists not as a private sphere within the world but as the community of those who live according to the new age already inaugurated in Christ. The danger of Constantinianism is that it blurs this distinction — the church becomes chaplain to the empire rather than an alternative to it.

notes.title: Nonviolence and the Triumph of the Cross
notes.content:
"The heart of the Sermon on the Mount is the conviction that the cross and not the sword, suffering and not brute power..."

"The triumph of the right is assured not by the might that comes to the aid of the right... The triumph of the right, although it is assured, is sure because of the power of the resurrection and not because of any calculation of..."

"He does not promise that if we turn the other cheek we will avoid being hit again. Nonretaliation is not a strategy to..."

"He gave them a new way to deal with offenders — by forgiving them. He gave them a new way to deal with violence — by suffering. He gave them a new way to deal with money — by sharing it."

"To be an apocalyptic people, that is, a people who believe that Jesus's refusal to accept the devil's terms for the world's salvation has made it possible for a people to exist that offers an alternative time."

Nonviolence is not a pacifist strategy calculated to be more effective than force. It is the form that life in the new age takes — a witness that the powers of sin and death are already defeated. The community shaped by the cross does not need to secure itself by violence because its existence is guaranteed by resurrection, not by military advantage.

notes.title: Truthfulness: Oaths in a World of Lies
notes.content:
"Bonhoeffer observes that oaths are a sign that we live in a world of lies."

"All of their words should be nothing but truth, so that nothing requires verification by oath. An oath consigns all other statements to the darkness of doubt."

"Only the cross as God's truth about us makes us truthful. Those who know the cross no longer shy away from any truth."

"Such a community of truthfulness cannot be afraid of conflict."

"The truth that constitutes the church means that the lies of the world cannot help but be exposed by the way..."

"Christian discipleship requires confrontation because the peace that Jesus has established is not simply the absence of violence. The peace of Christ is nonviolent precisely because it is based on truth and truth-telling."

The requirement not to swear oaths points to something deeper: the church is called to be a community so saturated by truthfulness that oaths become unnecessary. This is not a minor social practice — it means the church is formed to live without the lies by which societies sustain themselves. Truth-telling is therefore an act of resistance and a form of love.

notes.title: Wealth, Possessions, and the Freedom of Daily Bread
notes.content:
"Nothing enslaves more than that which we think we cannot live without. To be so enslaved, moreover, is to be captured by the powers of this world."

"Abundance, not scarcity, is the mark of God's care for creation. But our desire to live without fear cannot help but create a world of fear constituted by the assumption that there is never enough."

"Wealth stills the imagination because we are not forced, as the disciples of Jesus were forced, to be an alternative to the world that only necessity can create. Possessed by possessions, we desire to act in the world, often on behalf of the poor, without having to lose our possessions."

"To seek first the righteousness of the kingdom of God is to discover that that for which we seek is given, not achieved."

"The lure of wealth and the cares of the world produced by wealth quite simply darken and choke our imaginations. As a result, the church falls prey to the deepest enemy of the gospel — sentimentality."

Wealth is not merely a moral problem but an imaginative one: it creates a false sense of security that makes genuine dependence on God and neighbor feel unnecessary. Fasting and simplicity are disciplines that train the imagination to see the world as gift rather than resource, abundance rather than scarcity.

notes.title: Forgiveness as Political Alternative
notes.content:
"Jesus now proclaims that the kingdom of repentance has drawn near, breaking the cycle of murder as sacrifice by commanding those who would follow him to be reconciled before offering their gifts at the altar."

"Excommunication is not to throw someone out of the church, but rather an attempt to help them see that they have become a stumbling block and are, therefore, already out of the church. Excommunication is a call to come home."

"The willingness to be forgiven, which may require that I have my 'enemy' tell me who I am, is the only way that reconciliation can begin."

"To forgive and to be forgiven is not some crude exchange bargain to 'get on with life,' but rather to participate in a political alternative that ends our attempts to secure our existence through violence."

Forgiveness in Hauerwas is not a private spiritual transaction but the primary political act of the church. It breaks the cycle of retributive violence that organizes most human communities. We find it easier to forgive than to be forgiven precisely because forgiveness-received requires us to admit our dependence and culpability — it is the acknowledgment that we owe a debt we cannot repay.

notes.title: Jesus, Israel, and the Law: Not Law vs. Gospel
notes.content:
"It is not a question of law or gospel, but rather it is a question of Jesus; Jesus has come to draft us into the promise, into the story of Israel."

"When Christians presume that we are superior to the people of Israel, we ironically, like the Pharisees and Sadducees, claim a status rather than a calling."

"The people of Israel, the Jews, can never be 'left behind,' because if they are left behind Christians discover that we can make no sense of Jesus."

"Jesus is about Israel's redemption. Thus Paul in Rom. 9:30–33 elaborates John's appeal that God is able to raise up a people from the stones, because the stone turns out to be Jesus."

"The subversion is the righteousness that exceeds that of the scribes and Pharisees and as such is a subversion that will result in his crucifixion, for rather than violently overthrowing the old order Jesus creates a people capable of living in accordance with the new order in the old."

Hauerwas insists on keeping Israel and the church together: to abandon Israel is to abandon the story that gives Jesus his meaning. The question is never whether to follow the law but whether to follow Jesus — who is himself the fulfillment and embodiment of what the law was always pointing toward.

notes.title: Status vs. Calling: The Danger of Christian Privilege
notes.content:
"Those status seekers ask, 'Who is to be the greatest in the kingdom of heaven?' But that question itself is a stumbling block suggesting the need for a status that the kingdom does not invite."

"Identifying ourselves as Christians can be a form of protection from the recognition that we have become like the chief priests and Pharisees, whose assumption of responsibility for the tradition fills them with prideful presumption."

"This is a sobering list of failure and judgment, with descriptions of hypocrisy and failure in which we cannot help but see ourselves... We simply do not know how to live as a people who believe that Jesus is the resurrected Lord."

"His condemnations are harsh, but what could be worse than for the Pharisees and scribes, like any of us, to get the life they think they wanted?"

"Christians are tempted to rule like tyrants, believing that we possess the truth, a universal truth available without suffering."

The temptation of established Christianity is to turn the gift of the gospel into a possession, a status, a moral advantage over others. The Pharisees are not villains from a foreign religion — they are the mirror into which Christians must look. The church's calling is always received anew through discipleship, never possessed as heritage.

notes.title: The Grand Inquisitor: Bread, Freedom, and Caesar's Church
notes.content:
"The cardinal risks his own happiness in order that people may have the illusion of security as well as the happiness that is assumed to be the product of security."

"Feed them first, then ask virtue of them!"

"The church has managed to still the desire of freedom begun in Jesus by providing the earthly bread that people desire. The cardinal observes that the promise of freedom that Jesus proclaimed is too much for people."

"'For a long time — eight centuries — the church has not been with you but with him,' that is, with the devil."

Hauerwas reads Dostoevsky's Grand Inquisitor as the definitive account of what happens when the church takes the devil's third temptation: it trades the dangerous freedom of discipleship for managed security. The Inquisitor loves humanity — but in a paternalistic way that cannot trust people to be free. The church that "succeeded" by Constantinian means became the Inquisitor. The silent kiss Jesus gives is the only answer: love that refuses to coerce even in the name of welfare.

notes.title: God's Patience and the Hidden Kingdom
notes.content:
"The kingdom of heaven is like a mustard seed or yeast because to be drawn into the kingdom of heaven is to participate in God's patience toward his creation. Jesus is teaching us to see the significance of the insignificant."

"Apocalyptic names the time that requires waiting. It is not just any kind of waiting, but rather it is the waiting made possible by a hope made real."

"Without patience, those filled with hope threaten to destroy that for which they hope. Without hope, the patient threaten to leave the world as they find it."

"Rome knows how to deal with those who oppose it on its own terms. What Rome and all empires fear are those who refuse its terms of battle. Jesus has more time than Rome to engage in the work of calling into existence a people who have learned to live trusting in the goodness of God."

"We still live in apocalyptic time awaiting the return of the Son of Man. Jesus reminds the disciples and us that to faithfully witness to his peace there is always time to be patient."

The parables of the kingdom subvert our expectations about power and visibility. God works through the small, the hidden, the slow — the mustard seed, the yeast, the lost sheep. This is not weakness but a different kind of power: the patience of God who refuses to force. The church's task is to embody this patience, resisting the impatience that drives empires.

notes.title: The Passion: We All Killed Jesus
notes.content:
"The answer to the question of who killed Jesus, therefore, is that we all killed Jesus."

"Pilate killed Jesus because he had the responsibility to maintain order. 'The people as a whole' killed Jesus because they had nothing better to do. We all killed and continue to kill Jesus."

"In truth we stand with the Pilates of this world. We want God to be a king with armies. We do not want to give up our understanding of God as the one capable of putting everything right on our terms."

"We seek to explain these words of dereliction, to save and protect God from making a fool out of being God, but our attempts to protect God reveal how frightening the God of Jesus Christ is."

"'Christ's moment of most absolute particularity — the absolute dereliction of the cross — is the moment in which the glory of God, his power to be where and when he will be, is displayed before the eyes of the world.'"

The Passion narrative refuses every attempt to locate the guilt elsewhere. Pilate is all of us: we maintain our own order, we are bystanders with nothing better to do. And yet in this moment of total abandonment, God is most fully revealed. The cross is not God's absence but God's most particular presence — the God who refuses to stay safe even when it means dereliction.

notes.title: The Eucharist: Participating in Christ's Time
notes.content:
"It is significant that the righteous have not known that when they ministered, provided hospitality, and visited that they did all of this to Jesus."

"We do not remind the world of Jesus as if he is dead and our memory keeps him alive. The exact opposite is true; because Jesus lives we can be made participants in his time."

"The Eucharist is the feast that makes Christ's time the time in which his people live. Thus we say: 'Christ has died, Christ has risen, Christ will come again.'"

"Fear cannot dominate our lives if we have good work to do. 'Good work to do' is but another name for worship."

The Last Judgment scene in Matthew 25 is not about disguised charity: the righteous served Jesus without knowing it because their service was genuine — not performed for reward or recognition. The Eucharist is the practice that trains this unselfconsciousness: in receiving Christ's body we are drawn into Christ's ongoing life, so that our acts of service become participation in him rather than accumulation of moral credit. Time is not past memory but present participation.

notes.title: Evil as Privation, Cross as God's Truth About Us
notes.content:
"There can be no evil where there is no good... for any defect depends on the goodness that is always prior and therefore there is no evil apart from that which is good."

"The devil's only viable mode of operation is to 'tempt.' The devil can be only a parasite, which means that the devil is only as strong as the one he tempts."

"Our sin drives us mad because our very ability to revolt against our creator is dependent on the gifts we have been given by him."

"Only the cross as God's truth about us makes us truthful. Those who know the cross no longer shy away from any truth. Those who lie under the cross can do without the oath."

"Augustine learned that judging makes us blind because, as Bonhoeffer puts it, 'when I judge, I am blind to my own evil and to the grace granted the other person. But in the love of Christ, disciples know about every imaginable kind of guilt and sin, because they know of the suffering of Jesus Christ.'"

Evil has no independent existence — it is always a corruption of something good. This is not comforting in the face of real suffering, but it means evil is not an equal power opposing God. The cross is where God takes our capacity for evil fully seriously: it is the moment where God's truth about us (that we are sinners) and God's truth for us (that we are loved) coincide absolutely.
